topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt like an accusation in my hands. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon light slicing through the attic gloom, illuminating the faded ink of my father's signature. I had found it tucked inside a forgotten steamer trunk—a draft of a resignation letter, dated from thirty years ago. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. Every word scrawled on that page felt like a deliberate slight, a betrayal of everything I thought I knew about him. I crumpled the paper once, the sound sharp in the silence, then smoothed it out again, my knuckles white against the fragile surface. "You couldn't even finish it," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. The sheer audacity of it made my chest feel tight, a hot, constricting pressure behind my ribs.

[story 2]

I slammed the letter onto the mahogany desk, the sound echoing sharply through the empty study. It was a document from the early nineties, a formality I hadn't known existed until now, and it was utterly infuriating. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I stared at the precise, formal language, and a wave of burning resentment washed over me. I paced the length of the room, my footsteps heavy and uneven on the hardwood floor. "How could you just walk away like that?" I hissed to the empty air, my voice dangerously low and strained. The paper seemed to mock me, a tangible piece of evidence of a decision made without regard for anyone else. I kicked the leg of the desk, the sudden movement betraying the tremor running through me.

[story 3]

My fingers traced the faded script, a slow, deliberate movement that bordered on violent. This draft of a resignation—it was a snapshot of a life I never got to witness, a silent acknowledgment of a departure that felt like abandonment. A deep, churning anger settled in my stomach, cold and heavy. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of old photo albums. The crash was loud, a punctuation mark to my mounting frustration. I didn't look at the papers; I looked only at the empty space where my father used to sit. "All that time," I whispered, the sound ragged. The injustice of it, the casual way he could have simply walked out, fueled a furious energy that made my hands clench into fists at my sides.
